name: Aleron genetic must-do library
purpose: Maps a monogenic genetic finding (gene + classification + zygosity) to a routed clinical output: must-do (intervention/surveillance/mixed), carrier note, or risk-factor. Bypasses the QALY-ranked action map; emits to the must-do channel and re-weights action-library levers.

source.roster: Haverfield et al., BMC Medicine 2021;19:199 — peer-reviewed study of the Invitae proactive screening panel; enumerates 147 genes (the "~148").
source.actionability: ACMG SF v3.2 (Miller/Green 2023) — the 81 medically-actionable secondary-finding genes; the acmg_sf flag marks membership.
source.management_guidelines: NCCN, ACC/AHA, HRS, Endocrine Society, AASLD, MHAUS, FH Foundation/NLA, and condition-specific consensus, as cited per mustdo action.
source.commercial_panel: Invitae Comprehensive Genetic Risk Panel cites "up to 163 genes" (= 147 published + ~16 added since 2020, not yet built).

build: Two adversarial curate->verify workflows (ws01a4wsb: 42 ACMG-SF-core entries; widtt1l8b: 86 panel-expansion entries). All entries passed a second skeptical verification pass; corrections + watch-items retained in verifier_flags.

route_taxonomy.intervention: Personal MUST-DO. High penetrance + protocol mandates active treatment / risk-reducing procedure.
route_taxonomy.surveillance: Personal MUST-DO. Mandate is monitoring / early detection (incl. moderate-penetrance "consider enhanced screening").
route_taxonomy.mixed: Personal MUST-DO. Standing treatment AND surveillance co-equal.
route_taxonomy.carrier: INFORMATIONAL. Recessive single allele — reproductive/family relevance only; no personal mandate (mustdo empty; meaning in carrier_note).
route_taxonomy.risk-factor: INFORMATIONAL. Low-penetrance finding that informs situational decisions (e.g. peri-op/pregnancy prophylaxis for heterozygous thrombophilia) — NOT a standing order.

routing_gates: Gate 1 — actionable gene? (on panel / ACMG SF; else no entry)
Gate 2 — classification? (benign->drop; VUS->no action + reinterpret; P/LP->continue)

entry_field_notes.actionable_finding: PROSE description of the finding state that triggers the route. NOT yet a machine predicate — see TODO in GENETICS_INTEGRATION.md (classification + zygosity + variant-level special cases).
entry_field_notes.reweights: Human-readable hints of existing action-library levers this finding should re-rank when present. Need reconciliation to action-library lever IDs.
entry_field_notes.carrier_note: For recessive genes, what a single allele means; also flags X-linked carrier-female manifestation.
entry_field_notes.magnitude: One-sentence JUSTIFICATION for why the output is (non-)negotiable. Not a QALY ranking.
entry_field_notes.acmg_sf: On ACMG SF v3.2 actionable list.

caveats: Decision-support scaffold, not validated clinical software; pairs with genetic counseling.
Guideline cadences/thresholds must be pinned to the version in force at deployment.
Trigger predicates are prose, not executable (see integration TODO).
~16-gene delta to the commercial 163-gene panel not yet built.
Entries TTR, BTD, GAA are beyond this Invitae roster (carried from the ACMG-SF-core build).

multi_gene_entries.gene: MYH7, MYBPC3, TNNT2, TPM1, MYL2, MYL3, ACTC1

entries.condition: Hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome (BRCA1)

mustdo.action: Annual breast MRI starting age 25; add annual mammogram from age 30; clinical breast exam every 6-12 months
Discuss risk-reducing bilateral mastectomy as an option for breast risk reduction
Risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (no effective ovarian screening); recommended when childbearing complete
Prostate awareness for males; cascade testing of relatives

entries.magnitude: Lifetime breast cancer risk roughly 55-72% and ovarian roughly 39-44%; risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy substantially lowers ovarian-cancer and all-cause mortality, and intensive breast surveillance plus risk-reducing surgery markedly improve outcomes, making this standard of care rather than an optimization choice.

entries.condition: Hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome (BRCA2)

mustdo.action: Annual breast MRI starting age 25; add annual mammogram from age 30; clinical breast exam every 6-12 months
Discuss risk-reducing bilateral mastectomy as an option for breast risk reduction
Risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (no effective ovarian screening); recommended when childbearing complete
Prostate screening (PSA) for males from age 40; consider pancreatic surveillance only if family history and within a research/expert program; melanoma awareness; cascade testing of relatives
mustdo.cadence: annual PSA from 40; pancreatic surveillance per family history

entries.magnitude: Lifetime breast cancer risk roughly 45-69% and ovarian roughly 11-17%, with elevated male breast, prostate, and pancreatic cancer risk; risk-reducing surgery and intensive surveillance materially reduce cancer mortality, making management standard of care.

entries.condition: PALB2-associated hereditary breast cancer (with modestly increased ovarian and pancreatic risk)
entries.carrier_note: Biallelic PALB2 P/LP variants cause Fanconi anemia type N (FANCN), a distinct severe pediatric syndrome; the single-allele state addressed here is the adult cancer-predisposition phenotype, while biallelic status carries reproductive significance for couples who are both carriers.

mustdo.action: Annual breast MRI starting age 30; add annual mammogram from age 30; clinical breast exam every 6-12 months
Discuss risk-reducing mastectomy as an option, individualized by personal and family risk (not a standing recommendation)
Risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy is NOT recommended on genotype alone; consider only above age 45-50 in the context of family history of ovarian cancer
mustdo.cadence: individualized, only if family history; generally not before age 45-50

mustdo.action: Consider pancreatic surveillance (MRI/MRCP or EUS) only if there is a family history of pancreatic cancer, ideally within an expert program; cascade testing of relatives

entries.magnitude: Lifetime breast cancer risk roughly 40-60% (family-history dependent), which alone justifies the same enhanced breast surveillance and risk-reducing options as BRCA2; ovarian risk is only modestly elevated (about 3-5% lifetime) and pancreatic risk is small, so those are not free-standing surgical mandates and surveillance is what is non-negotiable.

entries.condition: ATM-associated hereditary breast (and pancreatic) cancer risk; biallelic = ataxia-telangiectasia
entries.carrier_note: A single P/LP allele also makes the person a carrier for autosomal-recessive ataxia-telangiectasia; biallelic offspring (if partner is also a carrier) are at risk for A-T (cerebellar ataxia, immunodeficiency, marked cancer predisposition, radiosensitivity).

mustdo.action: Annual breast MRI with contrast added to annual mammography (lifetime risk >20% supports MRI)
mustdo.cadence: annual, beginning age 40 (individualize earlier by family history)
mustdo.guideline: NCCN Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic

mustdo.action: CONSIDER pancreatic surveillance (MRI/MRCP and/or EUS) only if a first- or second-degree relative has had pancreatic cancer
Risk-reducing mastectomy is generally NOT recommended; manage by surveillance

entries.magnitude: Lifetime breast cancer risk is moderately elevated (roughly 20-30%); modest pancreatic and possibly prostate risk; the rare c.7271T>G missense allele confers notably higher breast risk than typical truncating variants.

entries.condition: CHEK2-associated hereditary breast cancer risk (modest, uncertain colorectal signal)
entries.inheritance: AD (heterozygous; biallelic/compound is rare and higher-risk)
entries.carrier_note: Generally treated as a dominant moderate-risk allele rather than a recessive carrier state; biallelic or compound-heterozygous carriers (e.g. two truncating alleles) carry substantially higher breast cancer risk and warrant individualized management.

mustdo.action: Annual breast MRI with contrast added to annual mammography (lifetime risk >20% supports MRI)
mustdo.guideline: NCCN Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic

mustdo.action: Colorectal screening per personal/family history; NCCN finds evidence INSUFFICIENT to intensify colonoscopy on CHEK2 status alone
mustdo.cadence: standard population screening unless family history dictates otherwise

mustdo.action: Risk-reducing mastectomy generally NOT recommended; no ovarian surveillance mandate

entries.magnitude: Lifetime breast risk roughly 20-30% (truncating variants higher than the low-penetrance c.470T>C / p.Ile157Thr missense allele); a colorectal association is reported but weak and not established enough to alter colon screening on genotype alone.

mustdo.action: CONSIDER enhanced breast screening (annual breast MRI added to mammography); NCCN notes evidence is limited and management is individualized rather than a firm MRI mandate
mustdo.guideline: NCCN Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic

mustdo.action: No risk-reducing surgery mandate and no ovarian-surveillance mandate; manage by breast imaging and family history

entries.magnitude: Breast cancer risk modestly increased (RR ~2, enriched for triple-negative disease) without clearly raising overall lifetime risk to high-penetrance levels; evidence base thinner than ATM/CHEK2, so enhanced screening is a CONSIDER.

entries.condition: BRIP1-associated hereditary ovarian cancer risk (biallelic = Fanconi anemia complementation group J)
entries.inheritance: AD (ovarian risk, heterozygous) / AR (Fanconi anemia group J, biallelic)
entries.carrier_note: Single P/LP allele also makes the person a carrier for autosomal-recessive Fanconi anemia group J; biallelic offspring (if partner is a carrier) are at risk for FA (bone marrow failure, congenital anomalies, cancer predisposition).

mustdo.action: Discuss risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) for ovarian risk reduction
mustdo.cadence: consider at age 45-50 or after childbearing complete
mustdo.guideline: NCCN Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic / SGO

mustdo.action: No established breast-cancer association; routine breast surveillance is not driven by this gene

entries.magnitude: Lifetime epithelial ovarian cancer risk moderately elevated (roughly 5-15%), enough to support risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy around age 45-50; breast cancer association not established.

entries.condition: RAD51C-associated hereditary ovarian (and triple-negative breast) cancer risk (biallelic = Fanconi anemia group O)
entries.inheritance: AD (cancer risk, heterozygous) / AR (Fanconi anemia group O, biallelic)
entries.carrier_note: Single P/LP allele also makes the person a carrier for autosomal-recessive Fanconi anemia group O; biallelic offspring (if partner is a carrier) are at risk for FA.

mustdo.action: Discuss risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) for ovarian risk reduction
mustdo.cadence: recommended by age 45-50 or after childbearing complete
mustdo.guideline: NCCN Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic / SGO

mustdo.action: CONSIDER enhanced breast screening (annual breast MRI added to mammography) given a predominantly triple-negative breast association

entries.magnitude: Lifetime ovarian cancer risk elevated to >=10% (roughly 10-15%), supporting RRSO by age 45-50; overall breast risk only modestly raised (~20%, RR ~2) and skewed to triple-negative disease.

entries.condition: RAD51D-associated hereditary ovarian (and triple-negative breast) cancer risk

mustdo.action: Discuss risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) for ovarian risk reduction
mustdo.cadence: recommended by age 45-50 or after childbearing complete
mustdo.guideline: NCCN Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic / SGO

mustdo.action: CONSIDER enhanced breast screening (annual breast MRI added to mammography) given a predominantly triple-negative breast association

entries.magnitude: Lifetime ovarian cancer risk elevated to >=10% (roughly 10-15%), supporting RRSO by age 45-50; overall breast risk only modestly raised (~20%, RR ~2) and skewed to triple-negative disease.

entries.condition: NBN-associated breast cancer risk (biallelic = Nijmegen breakage syndrome)
entries.inheritance: AD (modest breast risk, heterozygous) / AR (Nijmegen breakage syndrome, biallelic)
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP (evidence limited to the Slavic founder c.657del5)
entries.carrier_note: Single P/LP allele (classically the Slavic founder c.657del5 / p.K219fs) makes the person a carrier for autosomal-recessive Nijmegen breakage syndrome; biallelic offspring (if partner is a carrier) are at risk for NBS (microcephaly, immunodeficiency, radiosensitivity, lymphoid malignancy).

mustdo.action: CONSIDER enhanced breast screening (annual breast MRI added to mammography) for c.657del5 heterozygotes per NCCN
mustdo.guideline: NCCN Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic

mustdo.action: Evidence is limited to the c.657del5 founder allele; other NBN variants have no established actionable breast risk

entries.magnitude: Breast cancer association is weak and largely confined to the Slavic founder allele c.657del5; only modestly elevated risk, so screening is a CONSIDER rather than a firm mandate.

mustdo.action: Colonoscopy starting age 20-25 (or 2-5 yr before earliest family CRC)
Discuss risk-reducing hysterectomy +/- BSO after childbearing for women; consider daily aspirin for CRC chemoprevention
Upper endoscopy (EGD) with H. pylori test/treat for gastric/duodenal risk
Endometrial cancer awareness and prompt evaluation of abnormal bleeding (no proven screening test)

entries.magnitude: Lifetime CRC risk roughly 40-60% and endometrial roughly 30-50% in MLH1/MSH2; intensive colonoscopy with polypectomy substantially lowers CRC incidence and mortality, and risk-reducing gynecologic surgery markedly reduces endometrial/ovarian risk in women who elect it.

mustdo.action: Colonoscopy starting age 30-35 (MSH6) or 35-40 (PMS2)
Endometrial cancer awareness; discuss risk-reducing hysterectomy after childbearing (MSH6 elevated endometrial risk)
Consider aspirin chemoprevention

entries.magnitude: MSH6 and PMS2 carry meaningfully lower and later-onset CRC risk than MLH1/MSH2 (PMS2 lifetime CRC roughly 10-20%), so surveillance starts later with relaxed intervals, but colonoscopy with polypectomy still reduces cancer incidence and death.

mustdo.action: Colonoscopy starting age 20-25
Gynecologic risk management guided by deletion extent (endometrial risk approaches MSH2 only when the deletion extends into the MSH2 promoter)

entries.magnitude: EPCAM 3' deletions silence MSH2 and confer MSH2-like, predominantly colorectal Lynch risk; intensive colonoscopy prevents and detects CRC and reduces mortality, while endometrial risk depends on whether the deletion reaches the MSH2 promoter.

mustdo.action: Colonoscopy/sigmoidoscopy from age 10-15 (classic FAP); colectomy when polyp burden is no longer endoscopically manageable
Upper endoscopy (EGD with side-viewing duodenoscope) for duodenal/ampullary adenomas
mustdo.cadence: every 0.5-4 years per Spigelman stage, from age 20-25

mustdo.action: Annual thyroid ultrasound; clinical exam/surveillance for desmoids and other extracolonic manifestations

entries.magnitude: Classic FAP carries essentially 100% CRC risk by midlife from hundreds-to-thousands of adenomas; prophylactic colectomy is the central life-saving intervention, and duodenal surveillance addresses the leading post-colectomy cancer cause.

entries.carrier_note: A single MUTYH P/LP allele is a carrier state, mainly a reproductive/family consideration; monoallelic carriers have at most a modestly elevated CRC risk and do not require FAP-level surveillance.

mustdo.action: Colonoscopy starting age 25-30
Upper endoscopy (EGD with duodenal/ampullary inspection)
mustdo.cadence: every 0.5-4 years per Spigelman stage, from age 30-35

mustdo.action: Colectomy if polyp burden becomes endoscopically unmanageable

entries.magnitude: Biallelic MUTYH confers a substantial lifetime CRC risk (commonly cited roughly 40-80%) with a typically attenuated, later-onset polyposis phenotype; regular colonoscopy with polypectomy prevents most cancers and detects them early.

mustdo.action: Prophylactic total gastrectomy strongly recommended (typically age 20-30); endoscopic surveillance only if surgery is declined or deferred
mustdo.guideline: International Gastric Cancer Linkage Consortium / NCCN

mustdo.action: Breast MRI and clinical breast exam for lobular breast cancer risk in women

entries.magnitude: Diffuse gastric cancer in CDH1 carriers is often signet-ring and endoscopically occult until advanced; given substantial lifetime gastric-cancer risk and poor endoscopic detection, prophylactic gastrectomy is the definitive risk-reducing intervention, and women additionally face elevated lobular breast cancer risk.

mustdo.action: Small-bowel surveillance (video capsule or MR enterography) plus colonoscopy and upper endoscopy with polypectomy to prevent intussusception
Breast screening with MRI + mammography for women
Pancreatic surveillance (MRI/MRCP or EUS) and gynecologic/testicular evaluation per protocol

entries.magnitude: PJS carries very high cumulative cancer risk across GI, breast, pancreas and gynecologic sites plus childhood intussusception from hamartomatous polyps; multi-organ surveillance with polypectomy reduces both acute obstruction and cancer mortality.

entries.condition: Juvenile polyposis syndrome (JPS); SMAD4 also causes overlapping HHT

mustdo.action: Colonoscopy with polypectomy starting age 12-15 (or at symptom onset)
Upper endoscopy for gastric polyposis (SMAD4 carries high gastric polyp/cancer burden)
For SMAD4: screen for HHT - vascular malformations, brain/lung AVM imaging, iron studies for anemia

entries.magnitude: JPS confers substantially elevated lifetime colorectal and (especially SMAD4) gastric cancer risk; surveillance with polypectomy reduces cancer incidence, and SMAD4 carriers additionally need HHT vascular screening to prevent AVM-related catastrophic bleeds/strokes.

entries.condition: Polymerase proofreading-associated polyposis (PPAP) / oligopolyposis with microsatellite-stable CRC risk
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP germline variant in the exonuclease (proofreading) domain (e.g. POLE p.Leu424Val)

mustdo.action: Colonoscopy with polypectomy; begin early and repeat frequently given attenuated-polyposis phenotype
Upper endoscopy (EGD with duodenal/ampullary survey) given elevated upper GI/duodenal risk
Counsel on extracolonic risks (endometrial, possibly others) and tailor per personal/family phenotype

entries.magnitude: Exonuclease-domain POLE variants confer substantially elevated lifetime colorectal cancer risk plus excess duodenal and endometrial cancer, so dense colonoscopic surveillance from young adulthood is standard of care; precise penetrance estimates vary across the small reported cohorts.

entries.condition: Polymerase proofreading-associated polyposis (PPAP) — POLD1 form (oligopolyposis with CRC and endometrial risk)
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP germline variant in the exonuclease (proofreading) domain (e.g. POLD1 p.Ser478Asn)

mustdo.action: Colonoscopy with polypectomy for attenuated-polyposis phenotype
Upper endoscopy (EGD with duodenal survey)
Endometrial cancer surveillance and counseling in women; discuss risk-reducing hysterectomy after childbearing given notable POLD1-associated endometrial risk

entries.magnitude: POLD1 exonuclease-domain variants drive substantially elevated colorectal cancer risk and a particularly notable endometrial cancer excess (brain tumors reported but less established), justifying intensive colonoscopic plus gynecologic surveillance.

entries.condition: NTHL1-associated polyposis / tumor syndrome (NAP) — base-excision-repair adenomatous polyposis
entries.actionable_finding: biallelic P/LP variants (most commonly homozygous NTHL1 c.268C>T p.Gln90*)
entries.carrier_note: A single NTHL1 P/LP allele (heterozygous carrier) does not confer a personal polyposis/cancer mandate; it is a reproductive/family note — offspring are at risk only if the reproductive partner is also a carrier.

mustdo.action: Colonoscopy with polypectomy for adenomatous polyposis (applies to biallelic individuals)
Upper endoscopy (EGD with duodenal survey)
Surveillance/counseling for the broad extracolonic tumor spectrum (notably breast in women, plus endometrial and others)

entries.magnitude: Biallelic NTHL1 loss causes an MUTYH-like adenomatous polyposis with high colorectal cancer risk and a broad multi-tumor spectrum (breast, endometrial, others); heterozygous carriers carry no proven personal mandate.

entries.condition: MSH3-associated polyposis — recessive attenuated adenomatous/serrated polyposis
entries.carrier_note: A single MSH3 P/LP allele is a carrier state only — not a personal polyposis or Lynch-like mandate (MSH3 is not a classic Lynch gene); relevant as a reproductive/family note if a partner also carries an MSH3 variant.
entries.magnitude: Only biallelic MSH3 inactivation is linked to attenuated adenomatous/serrated polyposis and colorectal cancer; the evidence base is small and a single heterozygous allele carries no established personal risk, so the actionable trigger is the biallelic state, not carrier status.

entries.condition: Hereditary mixed polyposis syndrome (HMPS) — GREM1 upstream duplication
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous pathogenic GREM1 5' upstream duplication causing ectopic overexpression (founder event reported notably in Ashkenazi Jewish kindreds)

mustdo.action: Colonoscopy with polypectomy of the mixed (adenomatous, hyperplastic/serrated, juvenile-type) polyps
mustdo.cadence: every 1-2 years starting age ~25-30 (or earlier per family history)

mustdo.action: Counsel that surveillance is colon-directed; no established extracolonic mandate

entries.magnitude: The GREM1 upstream duplication causes a mixed-morphology colonic polyposis with substantially elevated colorectal cancer risk, warranting regular colonoscopic surveillance; the actionable lesion is a specific regulatory duplication, not a typical coding mutation.

entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP truncating variant (often with oligodontia/tooth agenesis as a clinical clue)

mustdo.action: Colonoscopy with polypectomy for adenomatous polyposis / elevated CRC risk (CONSIDER enhanced screening; evidence limited)
Note the oligodontia association and counsel on still-evolving cancer-risk evidence; some reports describe gastric/other GI risk

entries.magnitude: Rare AXIN2 truncating variants segregate with oligodontia plus colorectal polyposis/cancer, but penetrance estimates rest on few families, so enhanced colonoscopic screening is reasonable while the magnitude remains uncertain.

mustdo.action: Annual whole-body MRI plus dedicated brain MRI (Toronto protocol)
Annual breast MRI in women from age 20, with risk-reducing mastectomy discussion; avoid radiotherapy/CT where alternatives exist
Biannual clinical exam, annual abdominal/pelvic ultrasound, dermatologic exam

entries.magnitude: Near-complete lifetime multi-tissue cancer risk; Toronto-protocol surveillance is associated with a substantial survival advantage by catching tumors at curable stages, and radiation avoidance reduces iatrogenic second cancers.

mustdo.action: Annual breast MRI/mammography in women from age 30-35 (risk-reducing mastectomy is an option to discuss)
Annual thyroid ultrasound starting at diagnosis
Colonoscopy from age 35 every 5 years (sooner if polyps); endometrial awareness/sampling and renal imaging

entries.magnitude: Substantially elevated lifetime risk of breast, thyroid, endometrial, colorectal, and renal cancers; organized multi-organ surveillance shifts detection toward early, curable stages.

mustdo.action: Periodic abdominal MRI for renal cell carcinoma and pancreatic/adrenal lesions (resect renal tumors near a ~3 cm threshold)
Periodic plasma/urine fractionated metanephrines plus imaging for pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma
Annual ophthalmologic exam for retinal hemangioblastoma; brain/spine MRI for CNS hemangioblastomas

entries.magnitude: Very high penetrance for renal cell carcinoma, CNS/retinal hemangioblastomas, and pheochromocytoma; lifelong imaging with threshold-based resection reduces metastatic RCC and vision/neurologic loss.

mustdo.action: Annual biochemical screen (calcium/PTH, gastrin, prolactin, IGF-1, fasting glucose/insulin, chromogranin A)
Periodic pancreatic/duodenal MRI or EUS for neuroendocrine tumors and pituitary MRI
Treat primary hyperparathyroidism (parathyroidectomy) and gastrinoma/pNET per protocol

entries.magnitude: Near-complete penetrance for parathyroid, pancreatic neuroendocrine, and pituitary tumors; surveillance enables earlier treatment of pancreatic NETs, a leading cause of MEN1 mortality.

mustdo.action: Prophylactic thyroidectomy, timing stratified by RET codon / ATA risk category (infancy for highest-risk M918T)
Periodic plasma/urine fractionated metanephrines to exclude pheochromocytoma before any surgery/pregnancy
Periodic serum calcium/PTH for primary hyperparathyroidism (MEN2A)

entries.magnitude: Medullary thyroid carcinoma is near-inevitable; risk-stratified prophylactic thyroidectomy is preventive/curative, and metanephrine screening averts fatal intraoperative pheochromocytoma crisis.

entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP (SDHD and SDHAF2 are maternally imprinted — disease expressed predominantly with paternal transmission)

mustdo.action: Periodic plasma free or urine fractionated metanephrines/normetanephrines
Whole-body or skull-base-to-pelvis MRI for paraganglioma surveillance (SDHB carries the highest metastatic risk)
Resect/follow lesions surgically; for SDHB remain vigilant for malignancy, renal cell carcinoma, and GIST

entries.magnitude: Penetrance varies by gene (highest and most malignant for SDHB); early biochemical and imaging detection of paraganglioma/pheochromocytoma prevents catecholamine crises and metastatic disease.

mustdo.action: Serial exams under anesthesia from birth through early childhood to detect retinoblastoma at a treatable stage (affected offspring / at-risk infants)
mustdo.cadence: every few weeks to months in infancy, tapering

mustdo.action: Lifelong awareness/surveillance for second primary cancers (osteosarcoma, soft-tissue sarcoma, melanoma) in survivors; minimize radiation exposure

entries.magnitude: Childhood retinoblastoma is near-certain in germline carriers and eye-/life-saving outcomes depend on infant surveillance; for an adult carrier the personal must-do is mainly lifelong second-cancer awareness and radiation avoidance, while the acute pediatric mandate applies to at-risk offspring.

mustdo.action: Renal MRI every 1-3 years for angiomyolipomas; embolize or treat AML >3 cm with mTOR inhibitor to prevent hemorrhage
Brain MRI every 1-3 years (through ~age 25) for SEGA; baseline echo/ECG and serial neuro/epilepsy assessment
Pulmonary function and chest CT for LAM screening in women; dermatologic and ophthalmologic surveillance

entries.magnitude: Highly penetrant multi-system hamartomas; surveillance with threshold-based intervention (AML embolization, mTOR inhibitors for SEGA/AML) prevents renal hemorrhage and obstructive CNS complications.

mustdo.action: Annual brain MRI (including internal auditory canals) for vestibular schwannomas, from childhood/adolescence
Periodic spine MRI for meningiomas/ependymomas and annual audiology to time hearing-preservation intervention

entries.magnitude: Near-complete penetrance for bilateral vestibular schwannomas and other CNS tumors; serial MRI plus audiology times intervention (surgery, bevacizumab, radiosurgery) to preserve hearing and neurologic function.

entries.condition: WT1-related Wilms tumor predisposition (incl. WAGR / Denys-Drash / Frasier spectra)

mustdo.action: Renal (abdominal) ultrasound every 3-4 months until ~age 5-7 to detect Wilms tumor early (at-risk children)
Nephrology follow-up for associated nephropathy/proteinuria and genitourinary anomaly management

entries.magnitude: Elevated childhood Wilms tumor risk; quarterly renal ultrasound in early childhood detects tumors at a small, curable, nephron-sparing stage, with the acute mandate falling in childhood — for an adult carrier the relevance is mainly offspring surveillance and any associated nephropathy.

entries.condition: BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome (uveal/cutaneous melanoma, mesothelioma, renal cell carcinoma)

mustdo.action: Annual dilated ophthalmologic exam for uveal melanoma
Annual full-skin dermatologic exam for cutaneous melanoma and BAP1-inactivated nevi
Periodic abdominal MRI for renal cell carcinoma; clinical/imaging surveillance for mesothelioma; avoid asbestos exposure

entries.magnitude: Lifetime risk of one of several aggressive cancers (uveal/cutaneous melanoma, mesothelioma, RCC) is high, and early detection of uveal melanoma materially improves survival.

entries.condition: Hyperparathyroidism-jaw tumor syndrome / parathyroid carcinoma predisposition

mustdo.action: Annual serum calcium and PTH for primary hyperparathyroidism (high parathyroid-carcinoma risk)
Imaging surveillance for ossifying jaw fibromas and uterine tumors
Renal imaging for cysts/tumors

entries.magnitude: Primary hyperparathyroidism is highly penetrant and carries a notably elevated risk of parathyroid carcinoma, where early surgical management changes outcome.

mustdo.action: Annual full-skin dermatologic exam with dermoscopy
Strict photoprotection and patient self-skin-exam education
Consider pancreatic surveillance only if family history supports (less established than CDKN2A)

entries.magnitude: Rare activating CDK4 variants confer high melanoma risk comparable to CDKN2A, and surveillance plus photoprotection enables early melanoma detection.

entries.condition: Familial atypical multiple mole melanoma (FAMMM); melanoma-pancreatic cancer syndrome

mustdo.action: Annual full-skin dermatologic exam (consider every 6 months) with dermoscopy + photoprotection
Pancreatic surveillance (EUS/MRCP) typically when family history of pancreatic cancer present, from ~age 40
Smoking cessation (markedly amplifies pancreatic risk) and self-skin exams

entries.magnitude: Markedly elevated lifetime melanoma risk plus substantially increased pancreatic cancer risk; surveillance enables early melanoma detection and pancreatic screening in selected families.

entries.condition: DICER1 tumor predisposition syndrome (pleuropulmonary blastoma, thyroid, ovarian Sertoli-Leydig, others)

mustdo.action: Childhood chest imaging (CT/CXR) for pleuropulmonary blastoma per protocol
Thyroid ultrasound surveillance
Pelvic ultrasound for ovarian Sertoli-Leydig tumors and abdominal imaging per protocol

entries.magnitude: Most variant carriers stay healthy (incomplete penetrance), but childhood pleuropulmonary blastoma and several other tumors are detectable early, where surveillance improves outcomes.

entries.condition: Hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell cancer (HLRCC)
entries.carrier_note: Biallelic FH P/LP causes autosomal-recessive fumarase deficiency (severe infantile neurometabolic disease); a single allele in HLRCC context confers the dominant cancer phenotype, so this is a personal must-do, not merely a carrier note.

mustdo.action: Annual dedicated renal MRI (thin-section) for aggressive type-2 papillary RCC; do NOT defer to active surveillance if a renal lesion is found
Dermatologic exam for cutaneous leiomyomas; gynecologic evaluation for uterine fibroids
Refer renal lesions to a HLRCC-experienced center for prompt wide-margin resection

entries.magnitude: HLRCC-associated renal cancer is uncommon but unusually aggressive and metastasizes early, so dedicated annual MRI and prompt surgery are outcome-determining.

entries.condition: Birt-Hogg-Dube syndrome (renal tumors, pulmonary cysts/pneumothorax, fibrofolliculomas)

mustdo.action: Periodic renal MRI for chromophobe/oncocytic renal tumors
Counsel on spontaneous pneumothorax risk; avoid smoking and counsel on diving/altitude risk
Baseline chest CT to document pulmonary cysts

entries.magnitude: High lifetime risk of bilateral multifocal renal tumors plus recurrent pneumothorax; renal surveillance allows nephron-sparing surgery before metastasis.

mustdo.action: Earlier and ongoing prostate cancer screening with PSA, individualized
Shared decision-making with urology given elevated but moderate risk

entries.magnitude: Confers a several-fold increased prostate cancer risk that justifies earlier PSA-based screening, though it does not mandate intervention.

mustdo.action: Periodic abdominal imaging and endoscopy for multifocal GIST
Dermatologic/clinical evaluation for associated features (hyperpigmentation, mastocytosis, dysphagia)
Refer to GIST-experienced center; TKI therapy reserved for clinically significant tumors

entries.magnitude: Germline activating KIT variants cause highly penetrant multifocal GISTs that are detectable on surveillance and treatable, though management evidence is from rare kindreds.

mustdo.action: Annual biochemical screening (plasma/urine metanephrines) for pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma
Periodic whole-body or abdominal MRI for tumor localization
Pre-operative alpha-blockade and referral to experienced center if tumor found

entries.magnitude: Moderate lifetime risk of catecholamine-secreting tumors (often bilateral adrenal) that are curable when caught early but dangerous if undetected before surgery/anesthesia.

entries.condition: Hereditary papillary renal cell carcinoma type 1

mustdo.action: Periodic renal imaging (MRI/CT) for multifocal bilateral type-1 papillary RCC
Nephron-sparing surgery using a size threshold (~3 cm) at an experienced center

entries.magnitude: Moderate-penetrance, often indolent multifocal renal tumors where imaging surveillance and threshold-based nephron-sparing surgery preserve renal function and prevent metastasis.

entries.condition: Melanoma / renal cell carcinoma susceptibility (MITF E318K)

mustdo.action: Annual full-skin dermatologic exam and photoprotection given modestly increased melanoma risk
Consider renal imaging surveillance per family history (RCC association weaker)

entries.magnitude: The E318K allele confers a modest (roughly 2-fold) increase in melanoma (and possibly RCC) risk, supporting dermatologic surveillance rather than any intervention.

mustdo.action: Annual clinical exam (skin, neuro, BP for pheochromocytoma/renal artery stenosis, scoliosis)
Annual ophthalmologic exam in childhood for optic pathway glioma; MRI only if symptomatic
Breast MRI screening for women ages 30-50 (elevated breast cancer risk); evaluate growing/painful plexiform neurofibromas for MPNST

entries.magnitude: Near-complete penetrance with risk of optic glioma, MPNST, pheochromocytoma, and elevated breast cancer risk in women; structured surveillance catches treatable complications early.

entries.condition: Familial GIST / inflammatory fibroid polyp syndrome

mustdo.action: Periodic upper GI endoscopy and abdominal imaging for GISTs and inflammatory fibroid polyps
Refer to a GIST-experienced center; note many germline PDGFRA GISTs are imatinib-resistant (D842V) so surgery is primary

entries.magnitude: Germline activating PDGFRA variants cause multifocal GISTs and fibrous tumors detectable on surveillance, though management rests on rare-kindred experience.

entries.condition: POT1 tumor predisposition (melanoma, glioma, angiosarcoma, CLL)

mustdo.action: Annual full-skin dermatologic exam with photoprotection (melanoma is best-established risk)
Clinical awareness/low threshold to evaluate for glioma, angiosarcoma, and hematologic malignancy; no standardized multi-organ protocol yet

entries.magnitude: Elevated risk of a spectrum of cancers led by melanoma; dermatologic surveillance is justified though broader surveillance protocols remain undefined.

mustdo.action: Annual transthoracic echocardiogram for cardiac myxomas (most life-threatening feature)
Annual endocrine screening (cortisol/Cushing via PPNAD, IGF-1 for acromegaly, thyroid US)
Dermatologic and testicular/gonadal surveillance for lentigines and tumors

entries.magnitude: Highly penetrant with recurrent cardiac myxomas that can cause embolic stroke or obstruction and multiple endocrine tumors; echo surveillance is life-saving.

entries.carrier_note: Biallelic SDHA P/LP causes autosomal-recessive Leigh syndrome / mitochondrial disease; the single-allele dominant tumor risk here is a personal surveillance must-do, but reproductive counseling for the recessive metabolic phenotype also applies.

mustdo.action: Annual biochemical screening (plasma/urine metanephrines) for PPGL
Periodic whole-body MRI for paragangliomas and GIST/RCC surveillance

entries.magnitude: SDHA has notably low penetrance among SDHx genes, but the tumors it causes are dangerous, so low-burden biochemical and imaging surveillance remains warranted.

entries.condition: Rhabdoid tumor predisposition syndrome type 2 / small cell carcinoma of the ovary, hypercalcemic type (SCCOHT)

mustdo.action: Childhood brain/abdominal MRI surveillance for atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumors per protocol (largely childhood-limited)
Discuss risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for SCCOHT in females after childbearing
Refer to specialized center given aggressive, chemoresistant tumor biology

entries.magnitude: Associated tumors (rhabdoid tumors, SCCOHT) are rare but highly aggressive and chemoresistant, so surveillance plus risk-reducing surgery for SCCOHT is potentially life-saving.

entries.condition: Rhabdoid tumor predisposition syndrome type 1 (and schwannomatosis)

mustdo.action: Childhood brain and whole-body MRI surveillance for atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumors per protocol (childhood-limited window)
For schwannomatosis-spectrum variants, MRI and clinical surveillance for schwannomas and pain management
Refer to specialized pediatric oncology center

entries.magnitude: Germline SMARCB1 loss drives very aggressive early-childhood rhabdoid tumors (and a schwannomatosis phenotype); intensive early surveillance is the only lever, though it is high-stakes and time-limited.

mustdo.action: Annual biochemical screening (plasma/urine metanephrines) for pheochromocytoma (often adrenal/bilateral)
Periodic abdominal/whole-body MRI for tumor localization
Pre-operative alpha-blockade and referral to experienced center if tumor found

entries.magnitude: Moderate-penetrance, usually benign but catecholamine-secreting tumors that are curable when caught early and dangerous under anesthesia if undetected.

entries.condition: Familial hypercholesterolemia (heterozygous; homozygous/compound heterozygous = homozygous FH, far more severe)
entries.actionable_finding: Heterozygous P/LP in LDLR (loss-of-function), APOB (defective LDL clearance, classically p.Arg3527Gln), or PCSK9 gain-of-function = personal must-do. Two P/LP alleles (most often LDLR) = homozygous FH.
entries.carrier_note: These act dominantly, so a single P/LP allele IS the personal phenotype, not a carrier state. Caveat: PCSK9 loss-of-function variants LOWER LDL and are protective; only confirmed gain-of-function PCSK9 variants are actionable. Each first-degree relative has ~50% risk and warrants cascade lipid +/- genetic screening.

mustdo.action: Confirm with fasting lipid panel, then start high-intensity statin and treat to LDL-C target (at least 50% reduction and <100 mg/dL primary prevention, <70 mg/dL with ASCVD or major risk factors); add ezetimibe, then PCSK9 inhibitor and/or bempedoic acid to reach target
mustdo.cadence: Begin at diagnosis; recheck lipids 4-12 weeks after each change, then every 3-12 months
mustdo.guideline: AHA/ACC 2018 cholesterol guideline; NLA / FH Foundation

mustdo.action: Global ASCVD risk-factor control (BP, tobacco cessation, glucose); coronary artery calcium or other imaging may aid risk stratification in selected adults given lifelong LDL burden
Cascade lipid +/- genetic screening of first-degree relatives; screen children for FH (statin-eligible from ~age 8-10, earlier with strong early-ASCVD family history)
mustdo.cadence: At diagnosis; pediatric screening from age 2-10

mustdo.action: Homozygous/biallelic FH: refer to lipid specialist for combination therapy plus lipoprotein apheresis and/or evinacumab, with accelerated cardiovascular surveillance including aortic root/valve assessment

entries.magnitude: Untreated heterozygous FH carries roughly a 10-20x increased risk of premature coronary disease, with events often in the 40s-50s; lifelong LDL lowering to target substantially reduces that excess risk, and untreated homozygous FH causes coronary disease and events in childhood/adolescence, making early statin therapy one of the highest-yield interventions in genetic medicine.

entries.gene: MYH7, MYBPC3, TNNT2, TPM1, MYL2, MYL3, ACTC1
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP in a core sarcomere gene

mustdo.action: Baseline cardiology evaluation with ECG + transthoracic echocardiogram (cardiac MRI if echo windows poor or diagnosis uncertain)
mustdo.cadence: at identification; phenotype-negative carriers re-imaged every 1-2 yr in adolescence, every 3-5 yr in adulthood

mustdo.action: SCD risk stratification once HCM phenotype is present (family hx of SCD, unexplained syncope, NSVT, massive LVH >=30 mm, apical aneurysm, LV systolic dysfunction); ICD decision per the AHA/ACC risk-factor model (HCM Risk-SCD score informs, does not replace, judgment)
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis and every 1-2 yr thereafter

mustdo.action: Treat LVOT obstruction when present (beta-blocker / disopyramide / mavacamten / septal reduction); shared-decision counseling on vigorous/competitive exercise rather than blanket prohibition
Cascade genetic testing and serial clinical screening of first-degree relatives
mustdo.cadence: once for genetic testing; phenotype screening at intervals if genotype-positive

entries.magnitude: HCM is a leading cause of sudden cardiac death in the young; surveillance plus an appropriately placed ICD converts a potentially fatal first event into a survivable condition, and obstruction-directed therapy markedly improves symptoms.

entries.condition: PRKAG2 glycogen-storage cardiomyopathy (HCM phenocopy with pre-excitation and conduction disease)

mustdo.action: Baseline and serial ECG + echocardiogram for ventricular pre-excitation (WPW pattern), progressive AV block, and LV hypertrophy
EP surveillance for symptomatic bradyarrhythmia/AV block with low threshold for permanent pacemaker; manage atrial fibrillation and pre-excited tachyarrhythmias
Cascade testing and clinical screening of first-degree relatives

entries.magnitude: High-penetrance progressive conduction disease frequently culminates in advanced AV block; timely pacing prevents syncope and bradycardic death, the dominant avoidable harm in this syndrome.

entries.condition: LMNA dilated cardiomyopathy with conduction-system disease and malignant ventricular arrhythmia

mustdo.action: Serial ECG, ambulatory rhythm monitoring, and echocardiogram (cardiac MRI for fibrosis) to detect conduction disease, ventricular arrhythmia, and LV dysfunction
mustdo.cadence: at identification, then ECG/Holter and imaging roughly annually

mustdo.action: ICD evaluation at a LOWER threshold than other DCM (favor ICD over pacemaker-alone when pacing is indicated); apply the LMNA-specific risk model (NSVT, male sex, non-missense variant, AV conduction delay, LVEF <45%)
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis and as risk evolves

mustdo.action: Guideline-directed heart-failure therapy when LV dysfunction develops; cascade testing of first-degree relatives

entries.magnitude: LMNA is among the most malignant cardiomyopathy genotypes, with high rates of sudden death and rapidly progressive heart failure; early, lower-threshold ICD placement is the highest-leverage intervention.

mustdo.action: Echocardiogram plus cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement (arrhythmic risk tracks with myocardial fibrosis even at preserved EF) and ambulatory rhythm monitoring
mustdo.cadence: at identification, then imaging/rhythm monitoring every 1-2 yr

mustdo.action: ICD evaluation using arrhythmogenic-cardiomyopathy criteria (extensive LGE, NSVT, LV dysfunction) rather than EF alone
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis and as phenotype evolves

mustdo.action: Guideline-directed heart-failure therapy when indicated; cascade testing of first-degree relatives

entries.magnitude: Truncating FLNC carries a high burden of sudden arrhythmic death that can precede or exceed pump failure; fibrosis-guided ICD decisions help prevent otherwise unheralded fatal events.

entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP in a desmosomal gene
entries.carrier_note: rare biallelic desmosomal genotypes (e.g. recessive DSP/JUP cardiocutaneous forms such as Carvajal/Naxos syndrome) exist and are more severe; for these ACMG SF genes a single P/LP allele is itself an actionable autosomal-dominant finding, not merely a carrier state

mustdo.action: Baseline and serial ECG (including signal-averaged ECG), ambulatory/Holter monitoring, echocardiogram, and cardiac MRI (DSP/DSG2 often show left-dominant or biventricular involvement with LGE)
mustdo.cadence: at identification, then every 1-2 yr in phenotype-negative carriers
mustdo.guideline: HRS 2019 arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy / 2010 Task Force Criteria

mustdo.action: Exercise restriction - avoid competitive and high-intensity endurance exercise, which accelerates penetrance and arrhythmic risk in desmosomal variant carriers
Arrhythmic risk stratification and ICD decision once phenotype develops (prior sustained VT/VF, syncope, NSVT, RV/LV dysfunction, extensive LGE); cascade testing of first-degree relatives
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis and as risk evolves

entries.magnitude: Desmosomal arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy can present with sudden death as its first manifestation, often in athletes; exercise restriction plus phenotype-triggered ICD placement removes the most lethal, otherwise-silent risk.

entries.condition: Arrhythmogenic right-ventricular cardiomyopathy type 5 (TMEM43, p.Ser358Leu founder)
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP (notably the p.Ser358Leu founder variant; ACMG SF reporting is specific to known-pathogenic variants such as this founder allele)

mustdo.action: ECG, ambulatory rhythm monitoring, echocardiogram, and cardiac MRI; recognize highly penetrant, sex-modified disease (men affected earlier and more severely)
Prophylactic ICD strongly considered for male p.Ser358Leu carriers (and female carriers meeting risk criteria) given the distinctly malignant phenotype - a lower threshold than generic ARVC
Exercise restriction and cascade testing of first-degree relatives

entries.magnitude: The TMEM43 p.Ser358Leu founder variant is highly penetrant with very high lifetime sudden-death risk, especially in men; early prophylactic ICD has shown mortality benefit and is the decisive intervention.

entries.condition: Phospholamban cardiomyopathy (DCM and ARVC overlap, malignant arrhythmia)

mustdo.action: Cardiology/inherited-cardiomyopathy evaluation with ECG, echo and cardiac MRI (LGE) to characterize DCM/ARVC phenotype
mustdo.cadence: baseline then echo +/- MRI every 1-2 yr

mustdo.action: Arrhythmia surveillance with periodic Holter; risk-stratify for ICD given high SCD/malignant VA risk
mustdo.cadence: Holter every 1-2 yr; ICD per risk model

mustdo.action: Guideline-directed heart failure therapy if LV dysfunction; avoid competitive/endurance sport if ARVC phenotype

entries.magnitude: A high-penetrance arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy gene carrying substantial risk of progressive heart failure and sudden cardiac death, where ICD and HF therapy meaningfully change outcomes.

entries.condition: Desminopathy (DCM/ARVC/restrictive cardiomyopathy with conduction disease, +/- skeletal myopathy)

mustdo.action: Inherited-cardiomyopathy work-up: ECG, echo, cardiac MRI; assess for conduction disease and arrhythmogenic phenotype
Conduction/arrhythmia monitoring; low threshold for pacemaker/ICD given frequent AV block and ventricular arrhythmia
mustdo.cadence: ECG/Holter every 1-2 yr; device per risk

mustdo.action: Guideline-directed HF therapy; neuromuscular referral if skeletal myopathy features

entries.magnitude: Desmin variants cause a frequently arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy with conduction block where device therapy prevents sudden death and bradyarrhythmic events.

mustdo.action: Cardiology evaluation with ECG and echo (cardiac MRI as indicated) for DCM
Initiate guideline-directed HF therapy if LV dilation/dysfunction emerges; ICD per standard DCM risk criteria

entries.magnitude: A well-established, often highly penetrant DCM gene with progressive, frequently early-onset disease where early HF therapy and device assessment alter trajectory.

entries.condition: RBM20 dilated cardiomyopathy with high arrhythmic risk

mustdo.action: Inherited-cardiomyopathy evaluation: ECG, echo, cardiac MRI for DCM characterization
Arrhythmia surveillance with Holter; risk-stratify for early ICD given elevated malignant ventricular arrhythmia and SCD risk
mustdo.cadence: Holter every 1-2 yr; ICD per risk

mustdo.action: Guideline-directed HF therapy for LV dysfunction

entries.magnitude: RBM20 DCM is characteristically early-onset and arrhythmogenic with high sudden-death risk, justifying earlier ICD consideration than typical DCM.

entries.condition: Danon disease (X-linked) — severe HCM-like/DCM cardiomyopathy, skeletal myopathy, intellectual disability
entries.actionable_finding: hemizygous P/LP (males); heterozygous P/LP (females often manifest)

mustdo.action: Urgent inherited-cardiomyopathy evaluation: ECG (often pre-excitation/short PR), echo, cardiac MRI; affected males have early severe disease
Aggressive arrhythmia and HF management; early ICD and early transplant evaluation given rapid progression in males
Surveillance of carrier females, who manifest a later DCM/HCM phenotype

entries.magnitude: Danon disease causes rapidly progressive cardiomyopathy with very high mortality in young males, where early transplant referral is often life-saving; carrier females also manifest cardiac disease.

entries.actionable_finding: hemizygous P/LP (affected males); heterozygous P/LP (carrier females may develop DCM)

mustdo.action: Cardiology evaluation with echo and cardiac MRI; affected males develop progressive DCM and need scheduled cardiac surveillance
mustdo.cadence: echo/MRI every 1-2 yr, more often with disease

mustdo.action: Early ACE-inhibitor/ARB +/- additional HF therapy at first sign of dysfunction or per age-based protocol
Surveillance of carrier females for DCM, which develops in a meaningful minority

entries.magnitude: Dystrophin loss causes progressive dilated cardiomyopathy that is a leading cause of death in affected males; carrier females carry real DCM risk warranting cardiac follow-up.

entries.condition: Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy (X-linked) — DCM with conduction disease / atrial arrhythmia
entries.actionable_finding: hemizygous P/LP (males); heterozygous P/LP (carrier females may develop conduction disease/arrhythmia)

mustdo.action: Cardiology evaluation: ECG, echo; focus on conduction disease, atrial standstill, and atrial/ventricular arrhythmia
Low threshold for pacemaker/ICD due to high risk of AV block, atrial arrhythmia and sudden death
Surveillance of carrier females, who can develop conduction disease/arrhythmia

entries.magnitude: EMD-related disease carries high risk of conduction block and sudden death where timely pacing/ICD is protective; carrier females also manifest arrhythmia.

entries.condition: FHL1-related myopathy with hypertrophic/dilated cardiomyopathy and conduction disease (X-linked)
entries.actionable_finding: hemizygous P/LP (males); heterozygous P/LP (carrier females can manifest)

mustdo.action: Cardiology evaluation with ECG and echo for HCM/DCM and conduction disease; neuromuscular referral for myopathy
Arrhythmia/conduction monitoring with device therapy per findings

entries.magnitude: FHL1 variants can produce cardiomyopathy with conduction disease and skeletal myopathy; phenotype and penetrance vary, but cardiac surveillance enables timely device and HF management.

mustdo.action: Cardiology evaluation with ECG and echo (cardiac MRI as indicated) for HCM/DCM phenotype
Initiate phenotype-specific management (HF therapy, SCD risk-stratification) if cardiomyopathy emerges

entries.magnitude: An established but variably penetrant sarcomere-associated gene where surveillance catches HCM or DCM early enough to guide standard phenotype-based management.

mustdo.action: Cardiology evaluation with ECG and echo for HCM/DCM phenotype
Apply phenotype-specific HCM/DCM management and SCD risk-stratification if disease develops

entries.magnitude: A definitive but variably penetrant cardiomyopathy gene where periodic imaging detects HCM/DCM early for standard management.

entries.carrier_note: Biallelic TCAP loss-of-function causes autosomal-recessive limb-girdle muscular dystrophy (LGMD R7); a single allele is a recessive-myopathy carrier note for reproductive counseling.

mustdo.action: Cardiology evaluation with ECG and echo for HCM/DCM phenotype

entries.magnitude: TCAP has limited, lower-penetrance evidence as a dominant cardiomyopathy gene; periodic imaging is reasonable while heterozygosity also carries a recessive LGMD carrier implication.

entries.condition: VCL (metavinculin)-related dilated cardiomyopathy (also reported in HCM)

mustdo.action: Cardiology evaluation with ECG and echo (cardiac MRI as indicated) for DCM/HCM
Standard HF therapy and SCD risk assessment if cardiomyopathy emerges

entries.magnitude: An established but variably penetrant DCM gene where surveillance enables early HF therapy and device evaluation as phenotype emerges.

mustdo.action: Cardiology evaluation with ECG and echo (cardiac MRI as indicated) for HCM/DCM
Phenotype-specific management with SCD risk-stratification if HCM/DCM develops

entries.magnitude: A thin-filament sarcomere gene causing HCM (and some DCM) with variable penetrance; serial imaging supports timely HCM management and SCD risk-stratification.

entries.condition: TNNI3 (cardiac troponin I)-related cardiomyopathy (HCM, restrictive, DCM)

mustdo.action: Cardiology evaluation with ECG, echo and cardiac MRI for HCM/restrictive/DCM phenotype
HCM SCD risk-stratification and phenotype-specific therapy; note restrictive phenotype prognostic implications

entries.magnitude: A well-established sarcomere gene causing HCM and a recognized restrictive phenotype with variable penetrance; surveillance supports HCM management and SCD risk-stratification.

entries.condition: NKX2-5-related congenital heart disease with AV conduction disease (atrial septal defect, progressive AV block) +/- DCM

mustdo.action: Cardiology evaluation with ECG and echo for structural CHD (ASD) and AV conduction disease
mustdo.cadence: baseline echo; ECG every 1-2 yr for progressive AV block

mustdo.action: Conduction monitoring with pacemaker for progressive/high-grade AV block; surveillance for DCM
mustdo.cadence: ECG/Holter every 1-2 yr; pacemaker per findings

entries.magnitude: NKX2-5 causes congenital heart disease with progressive AV block carrying real sudden-death risk that pacing prevents, making conduction surveillance important.

entries.condition: Caveolin-3 disorders — rippling muscle disease / LGMD spectrum, with HCM and long-QT (LQT9) associations

mustdo.action: Cardiology evaluation with ECG (QTc) and echo for HCM and QT prolongation; neuromuscular referral for myopathic features

entries.magnitude: CAV3 is primarily a skeletal-muscle gene with variable, less-penetrant cardiac (HCM, LQT9) associations; baseline cardiac assessment with QT review is reasonable.

entries.condition: Alpha-B-crystallinopathy — myofibrillar myopathy with DCM/restrictive cardiomyopathy
entries.carrier_note: Some CRYAB variants cause autosomal-recessive disease (e.g. congenital cataract / fatal infantile myopathy); a single allele in those contexts is a reproductive carrier note.

mustdo.action: Cardiology evaluation with ECG, echo (cardiac MRI as indicated) for DCM/restrictive cardiomyopathy; neuromuscular referral for myofibrillar myopathy
Conduction/arrhythmia monitoring and standard HF therapy if cardiomyopathy develops
mustdo.cadence: ECG every 1-2 yr; ongoing if affected

entries.magnitude: CRYAB causes myofibrillar myopathy with a cardiomyopathy component of variable penetrance; cardiac surveillance supports timely device and HF management.

entries.condition: Delta-sarcoglycan: autosomal-recessive limb-girdle muscular dystrophy (LGMD R6) with cardiomyopathy; heterozygous reports in DCM
entries.carrier_note: A single SGCD P/LP allele is principally a carrier state for autosomal-recessive sarcoglycanopathy LGMD — a reproductive/family note, not a personal cardiac must-do; dominant DCM association from heterozygosity is weak/unproven.
entries.magnitude: SGCD is established as a recessive sarcoglycanopathy with associated cardiomyopathy; a single allele is essentially a carrier finding rather than a personal mandate, and the proposed dominant DCM link is not robust.

entries.condition: Congenital long QT syndrome (LQT1/KCNQ1, LQT2/KCNH2, LQT3/SCN5A)
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP with QT prolongation, symptoms, or family history of LQTS/SCD; a genotype-positive person with borderline/normal QTc (concealed LQTS) is still treated and trigger-counseled
entries.carrier_note: Rare recessive/digenic LQTS (e.g. Jervell-Lange-Nielsen, biallelic KCNQ1/KCNE1) causes a more severe phenotype with deafness; a single allele in that context is a carrier/family note. Standard autosomal-dominant LQTS acts from one allele.

mustdo.action: Baseline and periodic 12-lead ECG with QTc, plus exercise/Holter as indicated; lifelong inherited-arrhythmia/cardiology follow-up
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis, then annually or per symptoms

mustdo.action: Beta-blocker (nadolol or propranolol preferred), especially for LQT1/LQT2; mexiletine as gene-specific add-on for LQT3
Avoid QT-prolonging drugs (crediblemeds.org) and correct hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia; gene-specific trigger avoidance (exertion/swimming LQT1; startle/loud-noise and postpartum LQT2; sleep/rest LQT3)
ICD for survivors of cardiac arrest, and consider in recurrent syncope/VT despite beta-blockade; risk-stratify by QTc and prior events

entries.magnitude: Untreated LQTS carries a real risk of torsades-mediated syncope and sudden cardiac death in young, otherwise healthy people; beta-blockade plus trigger and QT-drug avoidance markedly reduces lethal events, making this a life-saving must-do.

entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous LoF P/LP with a spontaneous or drug-induced type 1 Brugada ECG pattern, or clinical/family criteria; an SCN5A variant without a type 1 pattern is not by itself diagnostic

mustdo.action: Inherited-arrhythmia clinic evaluation with serial ECG; risk-stratify on spontaneous type 1 pattern, prior syncope, and family history of SCD
Aggressive fever management with antipyretics (fever unmasks the phenotype) and avoidance of Brugada-aggravating drugs (brugadadrugs.org); limit excess alcohol
mustdo.cadence: lifelong; treat every febrile illness promptly

mustdo.action: ICD for survivors of cardiac arrest or arrhythmic syncope with type 1 pattern; quinidine and/or epicardial ablation in selected recurrent/refractory cases

entries.magnitude: Brugada predisposes to polymorphic VT/VF and sudden death, often during sleep or febrile illness in young adults; penetrance is genuinely variable and most genotype-positive people never arrest, but fever and drug avoidance plus ICD in high-risk patients are simple, life-preserving actions.

entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP with exercise/stress-induced ventricular arrhythmia or compatible clinical/family phenotype; genotype-positive relatives are treated even when the resting ECG is normal

mustdo.action: Exercise stress test and Holter to characterize arrhythmia; inherited-arrhythmia clinic follow-up (resting ECG is typically normal)
Non-selective beta-blocker (nadolol) as first-line standing therapy; add flecainide for breakthrough arrhythmia
Restrict competitive/high-intensity and emotionally stressful exertion; left cardiac sympathetic denervation and/or ICD for refractory or post-arrest cases

entries.magnitude: CPVT has high penetrance with adrenergically triggered VT/VF and substantial untreated mortality in the young; beta-blockade plus exertion restriction (and flecainide/denervation as needed) markedly reduces fatal events, so treatment is mandatory once the genotype is known.

entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP FBN1 variant (thoracic aortic aneurysm/dissection with systemic features)

mustdo.action: Transthoracic echocardiogram of aortic root/ascending aorta at diagnosis, repeat at ~6 months to establish growth rate, then serial imaging; baseline CT/MRA for full aortic assessment
mustdo.cadence: baseline + 6 months, then annually (or more often if enlarging)

mustdo.action: Beta-blocker and/or ARB (losartan) to slow aortic growth
Prophylactic aortic root replacement at ~5.0 cm (lower with rapid growth, family history of dissection, or before planned pregnancy); avoid isometric/contact/competitive sports
mustdo.cadence: surgery at threshold; activity restriction lifelong

entries.magnitude: Untreated Marfan aortopathy progresses to aortic root dilation and lethal dissection/rupture, historically shortening lifespan by decades; medical therapy plus threshold-based prophylactic surgery restores near-normal life expectancy, making surveillance and timely repair non-negotiable.

entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP in TGFBR1, TGFBR2, or SMAD3 with thoracic aortic and broader arterial involvement

mustdo.action: Baseline echocardiogram plus head-to-pelvis CT or MR angiography (arterial tortuosity and aneurysms extend beyond the aortic root); serial aortic and branch-vessel imaging
mustdo.cadence: baseline + ~6 months, then at least annually

mustdo.action: Beta-blocker and/or ARB (losartan) to slow aortic growth

Prophylactic aortic surgery at lower thresholds than Marfan, individualized by causal gene and features (often considered ~4.0-4.5 cm, earlier for TGFBR2/high-risk features); strict avoidance of isometric/contact/competitive sports
mustdo.cadence: surgery at gene-specific threshold; activity restriction lifelong

entries.magnitude: Loeys-Dietz tends to dissect at smaller aortic diameters and younger ages than Marfan with widespread arterial aneurysms, so untreated risk of early fatal dissection is high; whole-arterial surveillance plus earlier prophylactic surgery is what prevents premature death.

entries.condition: Familial/non-syndromic heritable thoracic aortic aneurysm and dissection (HTAD)
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP in ACTA2 or MYH11 (smooth-muscle-cell HTAD)

mustdo.action: Baseline echocardiogram and aortic imaging, extended to the full aorta by CT/MRA (dissection can occur in the descending aorta); serial surveillance imaging
mustdo.cadence: baseline, then annually (more often if enlarging or strong family dissection history)

mustdo.action: Beta-blocker and/or ARB for blood-pressure and aortic-growth control

Gene-specific prophylactic surgical thresholds (ACTA2 commonly considered ~4.5-5.0 cm, individualized to variant and family history; MYH11 less predictable); avoid isometric/heavy-resistance exertion and stimulant exposure
mustdo.cadence: surgery at gene-specific threshold; activity restriction lifelong

entries.magnitude: ACTA2 (and, less consistently, MYH11) causes heritable aortopathy where dissection can occur at near-normal diameters and younger ages; gene-informed surveillance with earlier surgical thresholds and BP control is what converts an otherwise silent dissection risk into a manageable condition.

entries.condition: Timothy syndrome / LQT8 (and CACNA1C-related Brugada syndrome)
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP (classic Timothy variant G406R, or other gain-of-function)

mustdo.action: Cardiology/electrophysiology referral; serial ECG with QTc and Holter monitoring
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis then at least annually
mustdo.guideline: HRS/EHRA/APHRS expert consensus on inherited arrhythmia

mustdo.action: Beta-blocker for QT prolongation; ICD for cardiac-arrest survivors or high-risk features
Strict avoidance of QT-prolonging drugs; correct electrolytes

entries.magnitude: Gain-of-function CACNA1C causes marked QT prolongation with high risk of life-threatening ventricular arrhythmia and sudden death, classically with multisystem Timothy syndrome.

entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP (loss-of-function affecting L-type calcium channel beta-2 subunit)

mustdo.action: EP/cardiology referral for clinical correlation; baseline ECG (consider high precordial leads) only if phenotype/family history present
Aggressive fever management with antipyretics; avoid Brugada-aggravating drugs

entries.magnitude: CACNB2 is a disputed, low-penetrance Brugada-susceptibility gene; an isolated variant without phenotype is of uncertain significance and mainly informs fever/drug precautions rather than mandating intervention or formal surveillance.

entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP in any of CALM1, CALM2, or CALM3 (typically de novo)

mustdo.action: EP referral; ECG/QTc, Holter and exercise testing to phenotype (LQTS vs CPVT)
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis then at least annually

mustdo.action: Beta-blocker; left cardiac sympathetic denervation and/or ICD for high-risk or refractory cases
Avoid QT-prolonging drugs (LQTS phenotype) and counsel on exertion (CPVT phenotype)

entries.magnitude: Calmodulin variants cause a severe, often early-onset arrhythmia syndrome (CPVT and/or extreme LQTS) with high rates of cardiac arrest and sudden death.

entries.condition: Calmodulinopathy — see grouped CALM1/2/3 entry (CPVT and/or LQTS)
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP (managed identically to CALM1/CALM3; see calmodulinopathy entry)

mustdo.action: EP referral and phenotyping (ECG/QTc, Holter, exercise test) — identical management to CALM1/CALM3
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis then at least annually

mustdo.action: Beta-blocker; LCSD and/or ICD for high-risk cases

entries.magnitude: CALM2 is one of three calmodulin genes that collapse to the same severe calmodulinopathy phenotype with high arrhythmic/sudden-death risk.

entries.condition: Calmodulinopathy — see grouped CALM1/2/3 entry (CPVT and/or LQTS)
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP (managed identically to CALM1/CALM2; see calmodulinopathy entry)

mustdo.action: EP referral and phenotyping (ECG/QTc, Holter, exercise test) — identical management to CALM1/CALM2
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis then at least annually

mustdo.action: Beta-blocker; LCSD and/or ICD for high-risk cases

entries.magnitude: CALM3 is one of three calmodulin genes that collapse to the same severe calmodulinopathy phenotype with high arrhythmic/sudden-death risk.

entries.condition: Catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia type 2 (CPVT2) — recessive
entries.actionable_finding: biallelic P/LP (two CASQ2 variants) for classic recessive CPVT2
entries.carrier_note: Classic CASQ2 CPVT is recessive: a single P/LP allele is predominantly a carrier state with minimal/no personal risk, carrying reproductive relevance if a partner is also a carrier (biallelic offspring would have CPVT2). Some heterozygous variants have been reported to confer a milder dominant CPVT phenotype, so an exercise stress test and family history are warranted before dismissing risk — a positive phenotype escalates a heterozygous carrier to intervention-level management.
entries.magnitude: Biallelic CASQ2 causes recessive CPVT with exercise- or emotion-triggered ventricular tachycardia and substantial sudden-death risk untreated; a single allele is usually only a carrier state, so the personal mandate applies to the biallelic (or phenotype-positive heterozygous) state.

mustdo.action: EP/cardiology referral for clinical correlation; baseline ECG (high precordial leads as needed) only with phenotype/family history
Aggressive fever control with antipyretics; avoid Brugada-aggravating drugs

entries.magnitude: GPD1L is a rare, disputed, low-penetrance Brugada-susceptibility gene; an isolated finding mainly informs fever and drug precautions rather than mandating treatment or formal surveillance.

entries.condition: Sick sinus syndrome / inappropriate sinus bradycardia (and reported Brugada/LV noncompaction association)
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP affecting the funny-current pacemaker channel

mustdo.action: Cardiology referral; ECG and Holter for sinus bradycardia/chronotropic incompetence; echo to assess for LV noncompaction
Pacemaker if symptomatic bradycardia or significant pauses develop

entries.magnitude: HCN4 variants most often cause bradyarrhythmia (sinus node dysfunction) that can require pacing, with a reported but less-established link to LV noncompaction.

entries.condition: Long QT syndrome type 5 (AD, disputed monogenic validity) / Jervell and Lange-Nielsen syndrome type 2 (AR, LQTS + congenital deafness)
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP for the disputed/low-penetrance dominant LQT5; biallelic P/LP for Jervell and Lange-Nielsen type 2
entries.carrier_note: A single P/LP KCNE1 allele has disputed/low-penetrance dominant LQT5 validity (ClinGen) and is also the carrier state for recessive Jervell and Lange-Nielsen syndrome type 2 — reproductive relevance if a partner carries KCNE1, and biallelic offspring would have LQTS with congenital deafness.

mustdo.action: EP referral; ECG with QTc, exercise/stress and family screening to establish whether a QT phenotype is expressed before labeling monogenic LQTS
Avoid QT-prolonging drugs and correct electrolytes; beta-blocker only if QTc is prolonged or symptomatic

entries.magnitude: Heterozygous KCNE1 has disputed dominant LQTS validity and low penetrance, with many carriers having normal QTc; chief mandates are QT-drug avoidance and treatment only if the phenotype is expressed, while biallelic disease causes severe JLN2.

entries.condition: Long QT syndrome type 6 (disputed gene-disease validity)
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP — but classic LQT6 designation is now disputed; KCNE2 better regarded as a drug-induced/acquired QT modifier

mustdo.action: Avoid QT-prolonging medications and correct hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia, especially periprocedurally
Baseline ECG and clinical correlation; do not over-diagnose monogenic LQTS on KCNE2 alone

entries.magnitude: ClinGen and subsequent curation disputed KCNE2 as a monogenic LQTS gene; an isolated KCNE2 variant mainly flags susceptibility to drug-/electrolyte-induced QT prolongation rather than a standing LQTS mandate.

entries.condition: Andersen-Tawil syndrome (LQT7) and short QT syndrome type 3 (SQT3)
entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP — loss-of-function causes Andersen-Tawil/LQT7; gain-of-function causes SQT3

mustdo.action: EP referral; ECG/QTc, Holter for ventricular ectopy/bidirectional VT, and assessment for periodic paralysis and dysmorphic features
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis then at least annually

mustdo.action: Beta-blocker and/or flecainide for ventricular arrhythmia; manage periodic paralysis (potassium, acetazolamide); avoid QT-prolonging drugs
ICD consideration for high-risk arrhythmia; for SQT3 phenotype, arrhythmia risk stratification

entries.magnitude: KCNJ2 Andersen-Tawil syndrome causes prominent ventricular ectopy/bidirectional VT with periodic paralysis and modest QT changes; arrhythmias are frequent but malignant sudden death is comparatively less common than in other LQTS, while gain-of-function variants cause SQT3.

entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP (null or glycine/splice; missense glycine substitutions higher risk)

mustdo.action: Celiprolol titrated to max tolerated dose to reduce arterial dissection/rupture risk (one RCT-supported agent; alternate beta-blocker if unavailable)
mustdo.guideline: vEDS international consensus + 2022 ACC/AHA aortic disease guideline

mustdo.action: Whole-body non-invasive arterial imaging (head-to-pelvis CTA/MRA or arterial duplex) for aneurysm/dissection surveillance
Wear medical-alert ID; avoid arteriography/elective vascular surgery and high-impact/strain activities; ensure emergency-team awareness of tissue friability
Manage pregnancy as high-risk (uterine/arterial rupture); maternal-fetal medicine co-management

entries.magnitude: Spontaneous arterial, bowel, and uterine rupture drive median survival to roughly the late 40s-50s untreated; celiprolol and avoidance of invasive procedures meaningfully reduce events.

mustdo.action: Screen for pulmonary AVMs (contrast echo bubble study; CT if positive) and treat by embolization to prevent stroke/abscess/hemorrhage
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis and every ~3-5 years / before pregnancy

mustdo.action: Brain MRI for cerebral AVMs at least once; screen/manage symptomatic hepatic and GI involvement
Manage recurrent epistaxis/anemia; iron-deficiency monitoring and replacement; antibiotic prophylaxis for procedures if untreated pulmonary AVM

entries.magnitude: Untreated pulmonary AVMs carry stroke and brain-abscess risk and cerebral AVMs carry hemorrhage risk; screening and embolization are high-yield preventive interventions. ACVRL1 carriers also have elevated PAH risk.

mustdo.action: Screen for pulmonary AVMs (contrast echo bubble study; CT if positive) and embolize to prevent stroke/abscess/hemorrhage
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis and every ~3-5 years / before pregnancy

mustdo.action: Brain MRI for cerebral AVMs at least once; evaluate symptomatic hepatic/GI involvement
Manage epistaxis/anemia with iron monitoring and replacement; antibiotic prophylaxis for procedures if untreated pulmonary AVM present

entries.magnitude: ENG/HHT1 carries the highest pulmonary and cerebral AVM burden of the HHT genes; untreated pulmonary AVMs cause paradoxical stroke and brain abscess that screening plus embolization largely prevent.

entries.condition: HHT type 5 / heritable pulmonary arterial hypertension (BMP9)

mustdo.action: Baseline echocardiography with PAH risk awareness; refer to pulmonary hypertension center if symptoms (dyspnea, syncope) or elevated estimated RVSP
mustdo.guideline: 2022 ESC/ERS PH guideline (general heritable-PAH approach); no GDF2-specific consensus

mustdo.action: If HHT phenotype present, apply HHT AVM screening pathway

entries.magnitude: GDF2 variants are a rare cause of heritable PAH and an HHT5 overlap phenotype; penetrance is low and no validated surveillance protocol exists, so management is symptom-triggered rather than a hard mandate.

mustdo.action: Periodic PAH screening (transthoracic echocardiography; consider exercise echo) in at-risk carriers, with referral to a PH center on any abnormality or symptoms
mustdo.guideline: 2022 ESC/ERS PH guideline (at-risk relative screening); no firm consensus

mustdo.action: Counsel on symptom recognition (exertional dyspnea, syncope) and avoid anorexigens/stimulants; reproductive counseling given pregnancy-associated PAH risk

entries.magnitude: BMPR2 is the commonest heritable-PAH gene but lifetime penetrance is only roughly 20% and higher in women; early detection can shift treatment to lower-risk disease, though no proven preventive therapy and no validated screening interval exist.

entries.condition: Heritable pulmonary arterial hypertension (and lipodystrophy spectrum)

mustdo.action: PAH symptom awareness and baseline/symptom-triggered echocardiography with PH-center referral on abnormality

entries.magnitude: CAV1 is a rare heritable-PAH gene with low penetrance and no gene-specific surveillance protocol; management mirrors the general at-risk PAH approach and is symptom-driven.

entries.condition: Heritable thoracic aortic aneurysm and dissection (HTAAD)

mustdo.action: Aortic imaging (TTE; CTA/MRA of full aorta) for aneurysm surveillance
mustdo.cadence: baseline then every 1-2 years per dimension/stability

mustdo.action: Strict blood-pressure control (beta-blocker / ARB) and avoidance of intense isometric/competitive exertion and stimulants
Prophylactic aortic surgery referral when diameter reaches gene-informed thresholds; note MYLK dissection can occur at near-normal diameters, lowering thresholds

entries.magnitude: MYLK is a definitive HTAAD gene where dissection can occur at minimally dilated aortic diameters, so surveillance plus aggressive BP control and a lower surgical threshold materially reduce fatal dissection risk.

entries.condition: Heritable thoracic aortic aneurysm and dissection (HTAAD)

mustdo.action: Aortic imaging (TTE; CTA/MRA of full aorta) for aneurysm surveillance
Strict blood-pressure control and avoidance of intense isometric/competitive exertion and stimulants
Prophylactic aortic surgery referral at gene-informed thresholds; PRKG1 is associated with young-onset dissection, lowering thresholds

entries.magnitude: PRKG1 causes aggressive, often young-onset thoracic aortic dissection sometimes at modest diameters; early surveillance, BP control, and a lowered surgical threshold are the levers that prevent dissection death.

mustdo.action: Aortic root/full-aorta imaging (TTE plus periodic CTA/MRA) and head-to-pelvis arterial imaging for arterial tortuosity/aneurysms
mustdo.cadence: baseline, then annually (more often if enlarging)
mustdo.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA aortic disease guideline; Loeys-Dietz management consensus

mustdo.action: Aggressive blood-pressure control (ARB and/or beta-blocker) and activity restriction (no isometric/contact/competitive sport)
Prophylactic aortic surgery at LDS-informed (lower) diameter thresholds; high-risk pregnancy management
mustdo.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA aortic disease guideline; LDS consensus

entries.magnitude: TGFB2/LDS4 carries aortic and branch-vessel aneurysm and dissection risk, generally less aggressive than TGFBR1/2 but still warranting close surveillance and earlier surgical thresholds than degenerative aneurysm.

mustdo.action: Aortic root/full-aorta imaging (TTE plus periodic CTA/MRA) and head-to-pelvis arterial imaging
mustdo.cadence: baseline, then annually (more often if enlarging)
mustdo.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA aortic disease guideline; Loeys-Dietz management consensus

mustdo.action: Aggressive blood-pressure control (ARB and/or beta-blocker) and activity restriction
Prophylactic aortic surgery at LDS-informed thresholds; high-risk pregnancy management
mustdo.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA aortic disease guideline; LDS consensus

entries.magnitude: TGFB3/LDS5 produces aortic aneurysm/dissection with variable, sometimes milder expressivity; surveillance and BP control with earlier surgical thresholds remain the standard preventive levers.

entries.actionable_finding: C282Y homozygote (p.Cys282Tyr / p.Cys282Tyr) with biochemical iron overload; compound C282Y/H63D only if iron overload is demonstrated
entries.carrier_note: A single C282Y allele, or H63D in any dose, is a carrier-level / low-penetrance state — it does not by itself cause clinical iron overload and is a reproductive/family-history note, not a personal must-do.
