mustdo.action: Baseline + serial transferrin saturation and ferritin; initiate therapeutic phlebotomy once iron overload is confirmed (elevated TSAT with rising ferritin)
mustdo.cadence: iron studies at diagnosis then annually if normal; once treating, phlebotomy targeting ferritin ~50-100 ng/mL

mustdo.action: Assess for end-organ involvement (LFTs; hepatic elastography/MRI, with biopsy considered if ferritin >1000 ng/mL or abnormal LFTs) given cirrhosis/HCC risk if untreated
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis and as ferritin/clinical course dictate

entries.magnitude: Biochemical penetrance to clinical iron overload is incomplete (highest in C282Y-homozygous men), but in those who do load iron, phlebotomy begun before fibrosis largely normalizes life expectancy, whereas untreated overload causes cirrhosis, HCC, cardiomyopathy and diabetes.

entries.condition: Hereditary transthyretin amyloidosis (hATTR — polyneuropathy and/or cardiomyopathy)
entries.actionable_finding: Heterozygous P/LP TTR variant (e.g. V122I/p.Val142Ile, V30M/p.Val50Met, T60A)

mustdo.action: Baseline and serial cardiac surveillance (echo with strain, NT-proBNP/troponin, ECG; bone-scintigraphy/PYP if cardiac signal) and neuro exam for small-fiber/autonomic neuropathy
mustdo.cadence: begin ~10 yr before predicted age of onset (variant-dependent), then annually

mustdo.action: On confirmed symptomatic amyloidosis, start disease-modifying therapy — TTR silencers (patisiran/vutrisiran/inotersen) for polyneuropathy, tafamidis (and emerging stabilizers/silencers) for cardiomyopathy
mustdo.guideline: FDA-approved labeling / ESC heart-failure & cardiac amyloidosis guidance

entries.magnitude: Untreated hATTR cardiomyopathy/polyneuropathy is progressive and fatal within years of symptomatic onset; stabilizers and gene-silencers slow progression and improve survival, so genotype-driven surveillance to catch the treatable window is the core value — though TTR is on ACMG SF v3.2 specifically for the cardiac (amyloidosis) phenotype.

entries.actionable_finding: Hemizygous male P/LP GLA variant; heterozygous female P/LP variant (X-linked — females are frequently affected, not merely carriers)
entries.carrier_note: Because GLA is X-linked with variable lyonization, a heterozygous female is not a benign carrier — she can develop significant renal, cardiac and cerebrovascular disease and warrants the same surveillance/treatment pathway.

mustdo.action: Baseline and serial renal (eGFR, albuminuria), cardiac (echo/strain, MRI, ECG) and cerebrovascular surveillance; confirm with leukocyte alpha-galactosidase A activity (males; unreliable in females, who need molecular testing) plus plasma/urine lyso-Gb3
Initiate disease-specific therapy — enzyme replacement (agalsidase) or oral chaperone (migalastat for amenable variants) — at evidence of organ involvement; manage proteinuria with ACEi/ARB
mustdo.cadence: at evidence of organ involvement, lifelong

entries.magnitude: Untreated Fabry leads to progressive renal failure, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and early stroke; enzyme replacement and chaperone therapy started before irreversible fibrosis preserve organ function, making early identification high-stakes — though disease-modifying benefit is greatest when started early and is less certain once advanced fibrosis is present.

entries.actionable_finding: Hemizygous male P/LP OTC variant; symptomatic/biochemically affected heterozygous female
entries.carrier_note: Heterozygous females span asymptomatic to hyperammonemic-crisis-prone depending on X-inactivation — a positive female is not a benign carrier and needs metabolic evaluation, dietary counseling and an emergency protocol.

mustdo.action: Metabolic-genetics referral; establish protein-restricted diet, nitrogen-scavenger therapy where indicated, and a written emergency hyperammonemia protocol (STAT ammonia during illness/fasting/post-partum)
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis, lifelong; ammonia STAT with any decompensation trigger
mustdo.guideline: expert consensus (urea cycle disorders consensus guidelines)

mustdo.action: Avoid known precipitants (prolonged fasting, high protein loads, valproate, systemic corticosteroids) and ensure a sick-day/stress plan

entries.magnitude: A single unrecognized hyperammonemic crisis can cause irreversible brain injury or death even in mildly affected individuals; recognition plus an emergency protocol converts a potentially lethal metabolic event into a manageable one, so this is must-do regardless of baseline symptoms.

entries.actionable_finding: Biallelic P/LP ATP7B variants (homozygous or compound heterozygous)
entries.carrier_note: A single P/LP ATP7B allele is a heterozygous carrier — heterozygotes do not develop Wilson disease and the finding is reproductive/family-screening relevant only.

mustdo.action: Confirm copper status (ceruloplasmin, 24-h urinary copper, slit-lamp for Kayser-Fleischer rings, hepatic assessment) and begin lifelong copper-lowering therapy — chelation (penicillamine/trientine) or zinc maintenance
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis; copper indices/LFT/CBC monitoring every few months early then 1-2x/year lifelong

mustdo.action: Low-copper dietary counseling and avoidance of copper-rich foods/water; monitor neurologic and hepatic status

entries.magnitude: Untreated Wilson disease progresses to cirrhosis, acute liver failure or neuropsychiatric degeneration and is often fatal, yet decoppering therapy started pre-symptomatically yields a near-normal lifespan — one of the highest-leverage genetic interventions in medicine.

entries.actionable_finding: Heterozygous P/LP variant in RYR1 (MH-associated; CACNA1S is a separate ACMG SF entry for the same indication)

mustdo.action: Flag MH-susceptible status in the medical record and to every anesthesiologist; strictly avoid all volatile inhalational anesthetics and depolarizing relaxants (succinylcholine)
Ensure dantrolene and an MH crisis protocol are available wherever triggering anesthesia could be given; offer family cascade testing

entries.magnitude: MH is a hidden trigger of a fulminant hypermetabolic crisis (historically high mortality, now low with avoidance + dantrolene) on first exposure to a triggering anesthetic — a largely preventable death that hinges on the chart flag; note ACMG SF reports only ACMG/AMP-classified P/LP variants, as penetrance for non-classic variants is incomplete.

entries.actionable_finding: Biallelic P/LP BTD variants conferring profound (or partial) biotinidase deficiency
entries.carrier_note: A single P/LP BTD allele is a heterozygous carrier with normal-to-mildly-reduced enzyme activity and no clinical disease — reproductive/family note only.

mustdo.action: Confirm with serum biotinidase enzyme activity, then start lifelong oral biotin supplementation (typically 5-20 mg/day for profound deficiency)
mustdo.guideline: expert consensus / ACMG newborn-screening ACT sheets

mustdo.action: Monitor for and reverse neurologic/dermatologic/auditory-visual signs; ensure adherence (lapses can cause optic atrophy, hearing loss, seizures)

entries.magnitude: Untreated profound biotinidase deficiency causes seizures, developmental regression, and irreversible hearing and vision loss; a few milligrams of daily biotin fully prevents this — a near-free intervention against permanent neurologic damage, though it is essentially a pediatric/newborn-screening diagnosis rather than an adult incidental finding.

entries.condition: Pompe disease (acid alpha-glucosidase / glycogen storage disease II)
entries.actionable_finding: Biallelic P/LP GAA variants (infantile-onset, or late-onset Pompe)
entries.carrier_note: A single P/LP GAA allele is a heterozygous carrier without disease — reproductive/family note only.

mustdo.action: Confirm with GAA enzyme activity (and CRIM status for infantile-onset); start enzyme replacement therapy (alglucosidase/avalglucosidase alfa) — urgent in infantile-onset, and at first evidence of muscle/respiratory involvement in late-onset
mustdo.cadence: at diagnosis; ERT infusions every 2 weeks, lifelong

mustdo.action: Serial respiratory (FVC, including supine) and motor/muscle surveillance; involve neuromuscular and pulmonary care

entries.magnitude: Infantile Pompe is fatal in the first year from cardiorespiratory failure without ERT, and late-onset Pompe progresses toward wheelchair and ventilator dependence; enzyme replacement transforms survival and function, making early diagnosis and treatment decisive.

entries.condition: X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy / adrenomyeloneuropathy (with primary adrenal insufficiency)
entries.carrier_note: Heterozygous females are NOT silent carriers: most develop adrenomyeloneuropathy-type spinal-cord disease by mid-to-later life, though adrenal insufficiency and cerebral disease are rare in them; daughters of affected males are obligate carriers and sons of carrier females are at 50% risk.

mustdo.action: Serial adrenal-function testing (ACTH, cortisol/cosyntropin stimulation) to detect Addisonian insufficiency early and start steroid replacement
Surveillance brain MRI in boys/young men to catch cerebral ALD in a window where hematopoietic stem-cell transplant (or gene therapy) is curative
Neurologic exam for adrenomyeloneuropathy (gait, spasticity) in adult males and symptomatic carrier females

entries.magnitude: Cerebral ALD is fatal if missed but transplant near-normalizes outcomes when caught early by MRI; undetected adrenal crisis is lethal, making surveillance genuinely life-saving.

mustdo.action: Lifelong avoidance of triggering anesthetics (volatile agents, succinylcholine); flag a documented MH-safe anesthetic plan in the chart and alert all surgical/anesthesia teams
Issue an MH-susceptibility alert (MedicAlert) and inform at-risk first-degree relatives

entries.magnitude: An MH crisis under triggering anesthesia is rapidly fatal without dantrolene, but is almost entirely preventable by trigger avoidance, so the actionable stakes are high despite low background event rate.

mustdo.action: Situational VTE prophylaxis around high-risk windows (major surgery, prolonged immobility, hospitalization, pregnancy/postpartum) rather than lifelong anticoagulation
Avoid added prothrombotic exposures where alternatives exist (combined estrogen contraceptives, estrogen HRT); counsel on VTE warning signs

entries.magnitude: Heterozygous prothrombin variant modestly raises lifetime VTE risk (roughly 2-3 fold) but most carriers never clot, so it informs situational decisions rather than mandating standing treatment.

entries.condition: Factor V Leiden thrombophilia (activated protein C resistance)
entries.actionable_finding: Factor V Leiden heterozygous (homozygous = higher risk)

mustdo.action: Situational VTE prophylaxis at high-risk windows (surgery, immobility, hospitalization, pregnancy/postpartum) instead of lifelong anticoagulation for heterozygotes
Avoid combined estrogen contraception/HRT where feasible; counsel on VTE warning signs; consider more aggressive management in homozygotes or compound heterozygotes

entries.magnitude: Heterozygous FVL is the commonest inherited thrombophilia and raises VTE risk a few-fold; absolute risk stays low and most never clot, so guidance is situational, not lifelong anticoagulation (homozygotes carry meaningfully higher risk).

entries.actionable_finding: hemizygous (male) P/LP variant; symptomatic heterozygous female
entries.carrier_note: Heterozygous females are obligate-risk carriers for affected sons (50%) and obligate-carrier daughters; carriers can have reduced factor IX and a real bleeding phenotype, so they warrant factor-level testing rather than reassurance.

mustdo.action: Manage through a Hemophilia Treatment Center: factor IX replacement / extended-half-life or non-factor therapy per severity, with a bleed and peri-procedure plan
Pre-procedure factor correction and avoidance of antiplatelet/anticoagulant agents and IM injections without coverage
Test factor IX level in heterozygous female relatives and offer reproductive/genetic counseling

entries.magnitude: Untreated severe hemophilia B causes disabling joint disease and life-threatening bleeds, while modern factor/non-factor prophylaxis largely prevents this, so confirmed disease is a clear treatment mandate.

entries.carrier_note: A single HAMP variant is a recessive-carrier / reproductive note and, like other non-HFE iron genes, can modestly modify iron loading when co-inherited with other variants; it is not a personal must-do on its own.
entries.magnitude: Biallelic HAMP causes severe early-onset iron overload with cardiac and endocrine failure, but a single allele carries no personal disease risk, so heterozygous findings are a reproductive note only.

entries.carrier_note: A single HJV variant is a recessive-carrier / reproductive note and may act as a modest iron-loading modifier with other variants; not a personal must-do alone.
entries.magnitude: Biallelic HJV produces aggressive juvenile iron overload (cardiomyopathy, hypogonadism) requiring intensive phlebotomy when affected, but heterozygotes are unaffected, so a single allele is a reproductive note only.

entries.carrier_note: A single LDLRAP1 variant typically causes little or no LDL elevation (true recessive), so heterozygosity is mainly a reproductive/family note rather than a personal lipid mandate.
entries.magnitude: Biallelic LDLRAP1 causes severe FH with very high LDL and premature coronary disease demanding aggressive statin/ezetimibe/PCSK9 therapy when affected, but single carriers are largely normolipidemic, so heterozygous findings are a family note.
reweights: lipid-panel context if biallelic or compound

entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP variant (biallelic = neonatal purpura fulminans)
entries.carrier_note: Biallelic/homozygous P/LP causes life-threatening neonatal purpura fulminans, so two carrier partners is a serious reproductive consideration even though the heterozygous personal route is risk-factor.

mustdo.action: Situational VTE prophylaxis at high-risk windows (surgery, immobility, pregnancy/postpartum); avoid estrogen exposure where feasible
If anticoagulation is ever started, bridge carefully to avoid warfarin-induced skin necrosis; counsel on VTE warning signs

entries.magnitude: Heterozygous protein C deficiency is one of the stronger inherited thrombophilias (VTE risk well above the common F2/F5 variants) but penetrance is still incomplete and many never clot, so it drives aggressive situational prophylaxis rather than lifelong anticoagulation after a single event (biallelic disease is a different, severe entity).

entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP variant (biallelic = neonatal purpura fulminans)
entries.carrier_note: Biallelic/homozygous PROS1 P/LP can cause neonatal purpura fulminans, so two-carrier couples warrant reproductive counseling even though the heterozygous personal route is risk-factor.

mustdo.action: Situational VTE prophylaxis at high-risk windows (surgery, immobility, pregnancy/postpartum); avoid estrogen exposure where feasible
Counsel on VTE warning signs; pregnancy thromboprophylaxis decisions in conjunction with hematology

entries.magnitude: Heterozygous protein S deficiency is one of the stronger inherited thrombophilias with incomplete penetrance, so it informs aggressive situational prophylaxis, not lifelong anticoagulation (assay interpretation is confounded by pregnancy/estrogen).

entries.actionable_finding: biallelic deficiency genotype (e.g., PI*ZZ, PI*SZ); PI*MZ heterozygous is a risk modifier
entries.carrier_note: Heterozygous PI*MZ is a mild risk modifier (modestly increased liver/lung risk especially with smoking) and a reproductive note; the personal disease mandate applies to biallelic deficiency (ZZ/SZ).

mustdo.action: Complete smoking avoidance/cessation and minimize occupational/inhalational lung exposures (the dominant modifiable driver of emphysema)
Pulmonary surveillance (spirometry +/- imaging) and consider IV augmentation therapy for established emphysema in severe-deficiency genotypes
Liver surveillance for fibrosis/cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma risk (LFTs, elastography/ultrasound); avoid alcohol excess

entries.magnitude: Severe-deficiency genotypes carry substantial emphysema and liver-disease risk that is heavily smoking- and exposure-dependent, so trigger avoidance plus organ surveillance (and augmentation) materially changes outcomes.

mustdo.action: Aggressive situational VTE prophylaxis at all high-risk windows (surgery, immobility, pregnancy/postpartum), recognizing this is the highest-risk inherited thrombophilia; involve hematology
Avoid estrogen-containing contraception/HRT; note heparin resistance may require antithrombin concentrate peripartum/peri-operatively
mustdo.cadence: at contraceptive, pregnancy, and procedural decisions

entries.magnitude: Antithrombin deficiency is the most thrombogenic inherited thrombophilia (notably higher VTE risk than FVL/F2), so while not a lifelong-anticoagulation mandate after a single event, it pushes hard toward prophylaxis at every risk window.

entries.actionable_finding: heterozygous P/LP variant (dominant; loss-of-function 'classic' vs gain-of-function type 4B)
entries.carrier_note: Unlike the recessive iron genes, SLC40A1 acts dominantly: a single P/LP allele can cause iron loading, so the finding is a personal surveillance item, not just a carrier note.

mustdo.action: Monitor iron indices (ferritin, transferrin saturation) and assess hepatic iron (MRI) for overload
Phlebotomy for iron overload, but cautiously in classic loss-of-function (macrophage iron loading) genotypes that tolerate phlebotomy poorly and can become anemic; tailor to genotype

entries.magnitude: Ferroportin disease can cause clinically meaningful iron overload, but penetrance and the safety/efficacy of phlebotomy vary by mutation class, so genotype-tailored surveillance rather than reflex aggressive depletion is appropriate.

entries.carrier_note: A single TFR2 variant is a recessive-carrier / reproductive note and may modestly modify iron loading with other variants; not a personal must-do alone.
entries.magnitude: Biallelic TFR2 causes an HFE-like (sometimes earlier-onset) iron-overload phenotype managed by phlebotomy when affected, but heterozygotes are essentially unaffected, so a single allele is a reproductive note.

verifier_flags.issue: Corrected mode from "mixed" to "surveillance". PALB2 has no standing treatment or surgical mandate the way BRCA1/2 do: risk-reducing mastectomy is an individualized option and RRSO is not recommended on genotype alone. The non-negotiable mandate is enhanced breast surveillance (annual MRI), so it is surveillance, not mixed/intervention.

Corrected penetrance_band from "high" to "variable". PALB2 breast penetrance is moderate-to-high and strongly family-history dependent (~40-60%), and ovarian risk is low-moderate (~3-5% lifetime). "Variable" is the honest band.

Corrected RRSO action and magnitude. Draft implied ovarian risk supports RRSO; NCCN states insufficient evidence to recommend RRSO on genotype alone and considers it only above age 45-50 with relevant family history. Ovarian lifetime risk is ~3-5% (vs ~1.5-2% population), not the substantial risk the original magnitude implied.

Tightened pancreatic surveillance language to require a family history of pancreatic cancer and ideally an expert/research program, per NCCN; the draft's "if family history" was acceptable but the qualifier on program setting was added for accuracy.

Tightened pancreatic surveillance phrasing to note it is reserved for those with family history and generally pursued within a research/expert screening program rather than offered to all BRCA2 carriers.

"mixed" mode is retained and correct: a standing risk-reducing surgical mandate (RRSO, and offered mastectomy) co-exists with intensive surveillance. Flagging for human awareness that some frameworks would call this pure intervention.
ACMG SF status confirmed: PALB2 was added in SF v3.1 and is retained in v3.2, so acmg_sf=true is correct for all three genes. Human should re-confirm against the exact SF v3.2 list in use.

Breast MRI/mammogram cadences and RRSO/mastectomy age windows match current NCCN; verify against the latest NCCN Genetic/Familial High-Risk: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic version in force at deployment, as age thresholds are periodically revised.

Corrected penetrance_band high -> moderate. Biallelic MUTYH is classed moderate in the house taxonomy and has an attenuated, later-onset phenotype; the ~40-80% CRC figure is real but the assigned band is moderate. Carrier handling (biallelic = actionable, monoallelic = carrier note) was already correct and is retained. ACMG SF = true confirmed.

Softened magnitude overclaim: 'risk-reducing gynecologic surgery largely eliminates endometrial/ovarian risk' -> markedly reduces risk in women who elect it. NCCN frames hysterectomy/BSO as a discussed/offered option after childbearing, not a universal mandate. Reworded the must-do accordingly.

Mode kept as 'mixed', but note: Lynch is fundamentally surveillance-driven (NCCN does NOT mandate prophylactic colectomy).
